past_medical_history: Episode of bilateral lower extremity numbness and tingling lasting 3 weeks, 8 months ago, attributed to lumbar radiculopathy at an outside clinic
family_history: No family history of multiple sclerosis or other neurological disease

social_history.occupation: Software engineer at a technology company
social_history.alcohol: Rare, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.exercise: Regular yoga and swimming, 3 times per week until recent symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact; able to spell WORLD backwards. Short-term and long-term memory intact. Mood mildly anxious, affect appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Visual acuity: right eye 20/60 (decreased from baseline 20/20 per patient), left eye 20/20. Right relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) present. Red desaturation noted in the right eye. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact. No facial weakness. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength full.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone mildly increased in bilateral lower extremities. Strength: upper extremities 5/5 bilaterally. Lower extremities: hip flexors 4+/5 bilaterally, knee extensors 5/5, ankle dorsiflexion 4+/5 on the right and 5/5 on the left, plantar flexion 5/5 bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased vibration sense at the right great toe and ankle. Light touch intact throughout. Pin-prick sensation mildly diminished below the T10 dermatome bilaterally. Joint position sense intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Achilles 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Right plantar response extensor (Babinski sign positive). Left plantar response equivocal.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing mildly impaired on the right with subtle dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic gait with circumduction of the right leg. Tandem gait impaired with lateral deviation to the right. Romberg sign positive.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive: electric-like sensation radiating down the spine and into the extremities upon neck flexion.

patient.chief_complaint: Painful vision loss in the right eye and progressive leg weakness over two weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 31-year-old left-handed woman of East Asian descent presents to the neurology inpatient service with a two-week history of progressive painful vision loss in the right eye and worsening bilateral lower extremity weakness. The symptoms began approximately 14 days ago when she noticed a dull, aching pain behind her right eye that worsened with eye movements. Over the following three to four days, she developed blurry vision in that eye, initially describing it as looking through frosted glass, which progressively worsened to the point that she could barely read her computer screen with the right eye alone. She also noticed that colors appeared washed out in the right eye compared to the left. Around the same time, she began experiencing heaviness and stiffness in both legs, more pronounced on the right side, with occasional tripping when walking. She reports that her legs feel clumsy, particularly going up stairs, and she has stumbled several times at home. She endorses a band-like tightness around her mid-abdomen that started approximately ten days ago. Additionally, she has noticed an electric shock-like sensation running down her spine into her arms and legs when she bends her neck forward. She reports increased urinary urgency over the past week, though she denies frank incontinence. On further questioning, she recalls a distinct episode approximately eight months ago when she experienced numbness and tingling in both legs from the knees down, accompanied by difficulty walking, which lasted about three weeks and then gradually improved. She saw a primary care physician at the time who attributed her symptoms to possible lumbar radiculopathy and prescribed ibuprofen. No imaging was performed during that episode. She denies any recent infections, fevers, rashes, joint pain, oral ulcers, or Raynaud phenomenon. She has not traveled recently and denies any tick exposures.

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular white matter, oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricles (Dawson fingers morphology)
findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 5 mm to 15 mm

signal_characteristics.T1: Hypointense (several lesions with T1 black holes indicating chronic demyelination)
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Two lesions demonstrate ring-like gadolinium enhancement indicating active inflammation

findings.location: Right retrobulbar optic nerve, extending approximately 12 mm in length

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Avid gadolinium enhancement of the right optic nerve

findings.mass_effect: Mild swelling of the right optic nerve

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Corpus callosum shows two small T2 hyperintense lesions along the callosal-septal interface
mri.impression: Multiple white matter lesions in characteristic distribution for demyelinating disease: periventricular (with classic Dawson fingers morphology), juxtacortical, infratentorial, and involving the right optic nerve. At least two lesions show gadolinium enhancement indicating active inflammation, while several T1 black holes indicate prior episodes of demyelination. The combination of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions satisfies dissemination in time by MRI criteria. The spatial distribution across periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and optic nerve regions satisfies dissemination in space. Findings are consistent with McDonald 2017 criteria for multiple sclerosis.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Classic Dawson fingers morphology, multifocal white matter lesions in typical MS distribution with both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions
Periventricular Dawson fingers pattern is atypical for NMOSD; optic nerve lesion is short-segment rather than longitudinally extensive
Lesion morphology and distribution are classic for demyelination rather than vasculitic ischemic changes

recommended_actions: Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
MRI cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to assess for spinal cord lesions
Serological testing to exclude mimics (NMO-IgG, MOG antibodies, ANA, B12, ACE)

Inflammatory markers.clinical_significance: Normal, argues against systemic vasculitis or active connective tissue disease
Normal, consistent with MS which typically does not elevate systemic inflammatory markers

Autoimmune and MS-specific.clinical_significance: Argues against systemic lupus erythematosus as a cause of CNS white matter disease
Argues against neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder
Low vitamin D is a known risk factor for multiple sclerosis and may contribute to disease activity

labs.interpretation: CBC and BMP are entirely within normal limits. Inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) are normal, which is typical for MS and argues against systemic vasculitis. ANA is negative, excluding lupus-associated CNS disease. Vitamin B12 is normal, ruling out subacute combined degeneration. NMO-IgG and MOG-IgG antibodies are both negative, effectively excluding NMOSD and MOGAD. ACE level is normal, arguing against neurosarcoidosis. Vitamin D is low at 22 ng/mL, which is a recognized modifiable risk factor for MS.
abnormal_values_summary: Vitamin D, 25-hydroxy: 22 ng/mL (low, risk factor for MS)

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands unique to CSF, not present in matched serum)

csf.interpretation: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (12 cells/uL, 92% lymphocytes) with mildly elevated protein. Five oligoclonal bands are present in CSF but absent in serum, confirming intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis. IgG index is elevated at 0.82. Myelin basic protein is elevated at 4.8 ng/mL, indicating active demyelination. These CSF findings are classic for multiple sclerosis and, in combination with the MRI findings, strongly support the diagnosis. Normal glucose and negative infectious studies argue against infectious etiologies.

Visual Evoked Potentials.clinical_significance: Prolonged P100 latency in the right eye confirms demyelination of the right optic nerve, consistent with acute optic neuritis
Reduced amplitude indicates axonal involvement in addition to demyelination
Normal latency in the left eye
Significant asymmetry supporting unilateral right optic nerve demyelination

output.interpretation: Visual evoked potentials demonstrate a markedly prolonged P100 latency (128 ms) and reduced amplitude (3.2 uV) in the right eye with normal responses in the left eye. The inter-eye latency difference of 24 ms is significantly abnormal. These findings confirm demyelination of the right optic nerve consistent with optic neuritis, providing additional evidence supporting the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.
abnormal_values_summary: Right eye P100 latency: 128 ms (prolonged, normal <115 ms)
Right eye P100 amplitude: 3.2 uV (reduced, normal >5 uV)
Inter-eye latency difference: 24 ms (abnormal, normal <6 ms)

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, dorsolateral, spanning less than two vertebral segments

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: No enhancement, suggesting chronic or subacute lesion

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T6-T7 level, dorsal columns
findings.size: 14 mm longitudinal extent, partial cross-sectional involvement

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Faint gadolinium enhancement suggesting recent or subacute activity

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (lesions each span less than 2 vertebral segments)
No cord compression or canal stenosis
Conus medullaris normal in position and signal
output.impression: Two short-segment intramedullary lesions at C3-C4 and T6-T7 levels, characteristic of demyelinating disease. The lesion morphology (short segment, partial cross-section, dorsal predominance) is typical of multiple sclerosis and atypical for NMOSD (which produces longitudinally extensive lesions spanning 3 or more segments). The T6-T7 lesion shows faint enhancement suggesting recent activity, which correlates with the patient's sensory level at T10 and lower extremity symptoms. The C3-C4 lesion is non-enhancing, likely representing a prior subclinical episode.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Short-segment, partial cross-sectional, dorsal-predominant lesions are classic for MS
NMOSD typically produces longitudinally extensive lesions spanning 3+ segments with central cord involvement

recommended_actions: Correlate with brain MRI and CSF findings for McDonald criteria assessment
Consider repeat imaging in 3-6 months to monitor disease activity

Optical Coherence Tomography.clinical_significance: Thinned retinal nerve fiber layer in the right eye indicates axonal loss from optic neuritis
Temporal quadrant thinning is the most sensitive marker for optic neuritis-related damage
Normal RNFL in the left eye
Ganglion cell layer thinning indicates retrograde neuronal degeneration from optic neuritis

output.interpretation: OCT demonstrates significant RNFL thinning in the right eye (average 72 um) with temporal quadrant most affected (48 um), consistent with axonal loss from optic neuritis. Ganglion cell layer thinning in the right eye (64 um) indicates retrograde neuronal degeneration. The left eye is within normal limits. These findings confirm structural optic nerve damage in the right eye and serve as a baseline for monitoring future disease activity and treatment response.

Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Excludes neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder with high specificity
Cell-based assay is the gold standard; negative result further excludes NMOSD

output.interpretation: Both aquaporin-4 and MOG antibodies are negative by cell-based assay, effectively excluding NMOSD and MOGAD. These results, combined with the short-segment spinal cord lesions and classic periventricular brain lesions on MRI, strongly favor a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis over other demyelinating diseases of the CNS.

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular white matter, previously noted Dawson fingers morphology
findings.size: Multiple lesions, stable compared to prior study

signal_characteristics.post_contrast: No enhancing lesions (previously enhancing lesions have resolved)

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, adjacent to the posterior horn of the right lateral ventricle

additional_observations: Previously enhancing periventricular and cerebellar lesions no longer enhance, indicating treatment response
Right optic nerve signal has improved though residual T2 hyperintensity remains
One new T2 lesion identified without enhancement, indicating subclinical disease activity
output.impression: Comparison with prior MRI shows resolution of gadolinium enhancement in previously active lesions, suggesting treatment response to acute therapy. However, one new non-enhancing T2 lesion has developed adjacent to the posterior horn of the right lateral ventricle, indicating ongoing subclinical disease activity despite treatment. This underscores the need for initiation or escalation of disease-modifying therapy. Right optic nerve signal has improved but residual changes persist.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Multiple sclerosis with ongoing disease activity
differential_by_imaging.rationale: New lesion development despite acute treatment confirms the relapsing nature of the disease

recommended_actions: Initiate or escalate disease-modifying therapy given evidence of new lesion activity
Repeat MRI in 6-12 months to reassess disease activity on therapy
Consider high-efficacy DMT given evidence of breakthrough activity

Pre-DMT Screening Labs.clinical_significance: Patient has VZV immunity; important before starting fingolimod or other S1P receptor modulators
Low JC virus antibody index indicates low risk of PML with natalizumab therapy
No latent tuberculosis; safe for immunosuppressive DMTs

output.interpretation: Pre-DMT screening labs are reassuring. Hepatitis B, C, and HIV serologies are all negative. VZV immunity is confirmed. JC virus antibody index is low (0.3), indicating low PML risk if natalizumab is considered. QuantiFERON is negative. Baseline immunoglobulin levels are normal. The patient is eligible for any disease-modifying therapy without contraindication from these screening results.

MS Disease Activity Assessment.clinical_significance: Moderate disability; impaired gait and visual function contribute to score
High relapse rate (extrapolated ARR ~3) suggests highly active disease requiring high-efficacy DMT

MS Disease Activity Assessment.value: More than 9 T2 lesions, 2 enhancing at baseline
MS Disease Activity Assessment.clinical_significance: High lesion burden with enhancing lesions indicates active inflammatory disease

output.interpretation: Disease activity assessment reveals moderate disability (EDSS 3.0) with a high relapse rate (2 clinical episodes in 8 months) and high MRI lesion burden with gadolinium enhancement. This profile indicates highly active relapsing-remitting MS. Current guidelines from the AAN (2018) and EAN/ECTRIMS (2024) recommend early initiation of high-efficacy disease-modifying therapy in patients with active disease. Appropriate first-line high-efficacy options include natalizumab, ocrelizumab, or ofatumumab. Given the low JC virus antibody index, natalizumab is a viable option with close monitoring.
abnormal_values_summary: High relapse rate: 2 attacks in 8 months
High MRI lesion burden with enhancing lesions

differential.key_distinguishing: AQP4 antibodies are negative. Optic nerve lesion is short-segment (12 mm) rather than longitudinally extensive. Brain lesions show classic Dawson fingers pattern atypical for NMOSD. Spinal cord lesions are short-segment rather than spanning 3+ vertebral segments.

MOG antibodies are negative. MOGAD optic neuritis typically presents with disc edema and bilateral involvement with better recovery. The periventricular Dawson fingers pattern is more typical of MS than MOGAD.

Normal ESR and CRP, negative ANA. MRI lesion morphology (ovoid periventricular with Dawson fingers) is characteristic of MS rather than vasculitic ischemic changes. CSF shows oligoclonal bands consistent with intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis rather than vasculitis pattern.

Normal ACE level, no leptomeningeal enhancement on MRI, no pulmonary findings reported. Sarcoidosis typically causes leptomeningeal or cranial nerve enhancement rather than periventricular white matter lesions.
Vitamin B12 level is normal at 485 pg/mL. B12 deficiency does not cause periventricular brain lesions or optic neuritis.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed neurological history and perform comprehensive neurological examination, establishing temporal and spatial dissemination of symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Two distinct clinical episodes (current optic neuritis and leg weakness, plus prior leg numbness episode 8 months ago) with multifocal neurological deficits suggesting CNS demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple periventricular ovoid T2/FLAIR lesions with Dawson fingers morphology, juxtacortical and infratentorial lesions, optic nerve enhancement, and both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions satisfying McDonald 2017 criteria for DIS and DIT

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs: CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, NMO-IgG, MOG antibodies, TSH, vitamin D, ACE level
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal CBC and BMP, normal inflammatory markers, negative ANA, normal B12, negative NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies, low vitamin D

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and infectious studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, oligoclonal bands present with elevated IgG index confirming intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis, elevated myelin basic protein

optimal_actions.action: Initiate treatment for acute optic neuritis with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acceleration of visual recovery and reduction of acute inflammation

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to assess for additional demyelinating lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment spinal cord lesions at C3-C4 and T6-T7 supporting dissemination in space

optimal_actions.action: Apply McDonald 2017 diagnostic criteria to confirm diagnosis of relapsing-remitting MS
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Criteria met: DIS (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) and DIT (enhancing and non-enhancing lesions simultaneously, plus two clinical episodes)

optimal_actions.action: Order pre-DMT screening labs: hepatitis B and C serologies, HIV, VZV IgG, JC virus antibody index, QuantiFERON-TB, baseline immunoglobulins
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All screening labs negative/normal, patient eligible for disease-modifying therapy

optimal_actions.action: Initiate high-efficacy disease-modifying therapy given highly active disease (e.g., natalizumab, ocrelizumab, or ofatumumab)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduction in relapse rate and new lesion formation

optimal_actions.action: Supplement vitamin D to target levels above 40 ng/mL
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Correction of vitamin D deficiency, potential modulation of MS disease activity

optimal_actions.action: Plan follow-up MRI at 3-6 months to establish new baseline on DMT and monitor for disease activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Assessment of treatment response and surveillance for new or enhancing lesions

optimal_actions.action: Refer to neuro-ophthalmology for visual monitoring and obtain baseline OCT
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Documentation of RNFL thinning as baseline, monitoring plan for visual outcomes

critical_actions: Perform brain MRI with gadolinium to identify demyelinating lesions and assess dissemination in space and time
Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for oligoclonal bands and IgG index
Test for NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4) and MOG antibodies to exclude mimics
Treat acute optic neuritis with IV methylprednisolone
Initiate disease-modifying therapy to prevent future relapses and disability progression
Apply McDonald 2017 criteria systematically to establish the diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Attributing symptoms to anxiety or functional neurological disorder without completing the diagnostic workup
Prescribing systemic chronic opioids for the eye pain without identifying the cause
Starting disease-modifying therapy without first excluding NMOSD (some MS DMTs such as interferon-beta and fingolimod can worsen NMOSD)
Performing brain biopsy when MRI and CSF findings are classic for MS
Delaying treatment with IV steroids for acute optic neuritis while awaiting outpatient follow-up
Rapid oral steroid taper without initial IV pulse therapy for acute MS relapse
key_reasoning_points: The combination of acute optic neuritis with a prior episode of myelopathic symptoms 8 months ago establishes clinical dissemination in time, a hallmark of MS
MRI findings of periventricular Dawson fingers, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord lesions satisfy dissemination in space per McDonald 2017 criteria
The presence of both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions simultaneously satisfies radiographic dissemination in time
CSF oligoclonal bands with elevated IgG index confirm intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis, supporting MS diagnosis and can substitute for DIT in McDonald criteria
Negative NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which have different treatment paradigms
Short-segment spinal cord lesions are typical for MS, while longitudinally extensive myelitis (3+ segments) would suggest NMOSD
Low vitamin D is a recognized modifiable risk factor for MS that should be corrected
Early initiation of high-efficacy DMT is recommended for patients with highly active disease to prevent irreversible disability accumulation
Lhermitte sign (electric shock with neck flexion) is a classic symptom of cervical spinal cord demyelination in MS
